results.possibleIdentity: Ludwig Redl is an Austrian-born sculptor and designer born on September 12, 1942, in Vienna. He was active in the late 1960s and 1970s, moving to California in 1965 at the age of 23. Redl's early artistic career was characterized by 'cryptic' sculptures made from alternative materials such as fiberglass, twigs, and thread, often drawing comparisons to the work of Eva Hesse. He lived and worked in the United States, the United Kingdom, and Brazil between 1965 and 2000. During the 1970s, he held solo exhibitions at notable venues including the Galerie George Givaudaun in Paris (1969), Cirrus Gallery in Los Angeles (1975), and Hal Bromm Gallery in New York (1975). He also participated in special projects at MoMA PS1 in 1977 and was a visiting sculptor and professor at UC Santa Barbara during the 1977-78 academic year. Later in his career, he transitioned into industrial design, serving as the Head of Design for Swarovski Crystal, where he collaborated with designers like Ettore Sottsass and Alessandro Mendini. While he was active in the same artistic circles as the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.)—specifically in Los Angeles and New York during the peak of the movement—his work evolved into large-scale metal and glass architectural installations and high-end industrial design.
